title: Ranunculus pilipes
taxonomy: Ranunculus pilipes is a species of flowering plant in the family Ranunculaceae. This plant belongs to the genus Ranunculus, which includes over 600 species of buttercups and their relatives. The Ranunculaceae family is part of the larger order Ranunculales, which also includes plants like the barberries and the columbines. Ranunculus pilipes is native to China, where it grows in mountainous regions. Its classification reflects its unique characteristics and evolutionary history as a distinct species within the Ranunculaceae family.
uses: Ranunculus pilipes, also known as the creeping buttercup, has been used in traditional medicine for various purposes. The plant's roots and rhizomes have been employed to treat conditions such as fever, rheumatism, and digestive issues. Its root and stem extracts have been utilized in some cultures for their anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties. However, its medicinal uses require further research to confirm their efficacy and potential side effects. More studies are needed to fully understand the benefits and limitations of using Ranunculus pilipes for medicinal purposes.

parts: Ranunculus pilipes is a plant species, but information about its medicinal parts is limited. It is native to China and belongs to the buttercup family. side_effects: Ranunculus pilipes, also known as the hairy-ranunculus, is a plant that contains toxic compounds, including ranunculin and other glycosides. These compounds can cause skin irritation, burning, and blistering if the plant's sap comes into contact with skin. Ingesting any part of the plant can lead to more severe symptoms, including nausea, vomiting, and diarrhea. The sap can also cause eye irritation and temporary blindness if it gets into the eyes. Prolonged exposure to the plant's toxic compounds can cause more serious health issues, including organ damage.
